title: Built a Cognitive LLM trading agent but curious how to approach scaling.
author: Tight-Pepper-4721
selftext:
For the past few months, I’ve been building a trading agent with an LLM backend in Python. The system currently runs in simulation, includes memory, risk controls, strategy adaptation, and enough supporting infrastructure that it’s no longer just a weekend experiment.

The challenge now isn’t getting it to work.  
The challenge is figuring out how to scale it.

Things that seemed straightforward at first—state management, context handling, compute costs, broker abstraction, evaluation frameworks, multi-agent architectures, reliability, and deployment—have become engineering problems in their own right.

I’m sure I’m not the only one running into these issues.

Are there other builders, engineers, researchers, founders, or creators here who are working on similar systems? Or who have experience taking projects beyond the proof-of-concept stage?

I’d be especially interested in hearing from people who have dealt with scaling agent-based systems, whether from a technical, product, or startup perspective.

I’m also open to connecting with others who may want to collaborate, exchange ideas, or explore ways to take projects like these beyond the solo-builder phase.

No sales pitch. No signal-sharing groups.

Just looking to connect with like-minded people working on difficult problems and see where the conversation goes.

If this sounds familiar, feel free to comment or send me a DM.

comments.author: Whakomatic
comments.body: These are all things that used to take teams of people and measured in months. The help I can offer now is that you don’t need as much as you think. Software ran just fine in the days before all those systems and layers. Focus on output over process.

replies.author: Tight-Pepper-4721
replies.body:
Okay 
Would love to connect and maybe discuss more

comments.author: AttitudeGrouchy33
comments.body:
Founder of milo here. We’ve been working on a similar problem from the product side: how do you take trading agents from “cool demo” to something a normal person can actually trust with capital?

The scaling problems we keep coming back to are less about the model and more about the operating system around it:

- deterministic account state, not model-owned state
- strict risk layer that can reject the agent
- strategy/playbook versioning
- append-only trade/reasoning logs
- broker/wallet abstraction
- clear user controls: pause, change risk, fire agent
- evaluation that separates market regime from agent behavior

The hard part is not making an LLM say smart things about markets. The hard part is making the entire loop boring, inspectable, recoverable, and safe enough to run repeatedly.

Happy to compare notes. This category is early and most of the real work is infrastructure, not prompts.

replies.author: Tight-Pepper-4721
replies.body:
Yess true 
That’s why I started with the fundamental concept of establishing a strict risk layer , adding proofs at every layer and also using mathematical functions at each step to prevent hallucinations or any mis trades get cut off instantly 
So in terms of risk management of the capital , I’ve tried to be very very careful 
Cause in finance not losing money comes before growing it
